name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١)
```

```english
Indeed We sent Noah to his people, [saying,] ‘Warn your people before a painful punishment overtakes them.’ (1)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘O my people! Indeed, I am a manifest warner to you. (2)
```

```arabic
أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٣)
```

```english
Worship Allah and be wary of Him, and obey me, (3)
```

```arabic
يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ إِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا جَآءَ لَا يُؤَخَّرُ ۖ لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
```

```english
that He may forgive you some of your sins and respite you until a specified time. Indeed when Allah’s [appointed] time comes, it cannot be deferred, if you know.’ (4)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِى لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا (٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed, I have summoned my people night and day (5)
```

```arabic
فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعَآءِىٓ إِلَّا فِرَارًا (٦)
```

```english
but my summons only increases their evasion. (6)
```

```arabic
وَإِنِّى كُلَّمَا دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوٓا۟ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَٱسْتَغْشَوْا۟ ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ ٱسْتِكْبَارًا (٧)
```

```english
Indeed whenever I have summoned them, so that You might forgive them, they would put their fingers into their ears and draw their cloaks over their heads, and they were persistent [in their unfaith], and disdainful in [their] arrogance. (7)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهَارًا (٨)
```

```english
Again I summoned them aloud, (8)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَنتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا (٩)
```

```english
and again appealed to them publicly and confided with them privately, (9)
```

```arabic
فَقُلْتُ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفَّارًا (١٠)
```

```english
telling [them]: ‘‘Plead to your Lord for forgiveness. Indeed, He is all-forgiving. (10)
```

```arabic
يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا (١١)
```

```english
He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, (11)
```

```arabic
وَيُمْدِدْكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ أَنْهَـٰرًا (١٢)
```

```english
and aid you with wealth and sons, and provide you with gardens and provide you with streams. (12)
```

```arabic
مَّا لَكُمْ لَا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقَارًا (١٣)
```

```english
What is the matter with you that you do not look upon Allah with veneration, (13)
```

```arabic
وَقَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْوَارًا (١٤)
```

```english
though He has created you in [various] stages? (14)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا (١٥)
```

```english
Have you not seen how Allah has created the seven heavens in layers, (15)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلَ ٱلْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًا (١٦)
```

```english
and has made therein the moon for a light and the sun for a lamp? (16)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ أَنۢبَتَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا (١٧)
```

```english
Allah made you grow from the earth, with a [vegetable] growth. (17)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيهَا وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْرَاجًا (١٨)
```

```english
Then He makes you return to it, and He will bring you forth [without fail]. (18)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ بِسَاطًا (١٩)
```

```english
Allah has made the earth a vast expanse for you (19)
```

```arabic
لِّتَسْلُكُوا۟ مِنْهَا سُبُلًا فِجَاجًا (٢٠)
```

```english
so that you may travel over its spacious ways.’’ ’ (20)
```

```arabic
قَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِى وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَوَلَدُهُۥٓ إِلَّا خَسَارًا (٢١)
```

```english
Noah said, ‘My Lord! They have disobeyed me, following someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss, (21)
```

```arabic
وَمَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا (٢٢)
```

```english
and they have devised an outrageous plot. (22)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَذَرُنَّ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا وَلَا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْرًا (٢٣)
```

```english
They say, ‘‘Do not abandon your gods. Do not abandon Wadd, nor Suwa, nor Yaghuth, Ya‘uq and Nasr,’’ (23)
```

```arabic
وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلَـٰلًا (٢٤)
```

```english
and already they have led many astray. Do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but error.’ (24)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah points to the mission of Nuh (peace be upon him) to his people, the summary of his call, and their lack of response to him. It then mentions his complaint to his Lord regarding them, his supplication against them, and his seeking of forgiveness for himself, his parents, for whoever entered his house as a believer, and for the faithful (المؤمنين) men and women. Subsequently, it describes the descent of punishment upon them and their destruction by drowning. The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى قَوْمِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — "Indeed, We sent Noah to his people, [saying], ‘Warn your people before there comes to them a painful punishment.’" (71:1). The phrase "Warn your people" (أَنْ أَنْذِرْ قَوْمَكَ) etc., is an explanation of his mission; meaning: We revealed to him to warn, etc.

In this speech, there is an indication that his people were exposed to punishment due to their polytheism (الشرك) and their acts of disobedience (المعاصي), as indicated by what is narrated of his saying (peace be upon him) in the following verse: "Worship Allah and be wary of Him." This is because warning (الإنذار) is a form of frightening (تخويف), and frightening only occurs regarding a potential danger that cannot be averted except through caution (التحذر). His saying: "before there comes to them a painful punishment" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ) conveys that the punishment was heading toward them and would not leave them unless they took caution against it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ** — "He said, ‘O my people! Indeed I am a manifest warner to you, [announcing] that you should worship Allah and be wary of Him, and obey me.’" (71:2-3). This is an exposition of the delivery of his mission, summarized by his saying: "Indeed I am a manifest warner to you" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ), and detailed by his saying: "that you should worship Allah" (أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ), etc.

In his attributing "the people" to himself [by saying "O my people"], there is a manifestation of compassion and mercy; meaning: you are my people, our national community unites us, what harms you harms me, and I desire nothing for you but your good and happiness; indeed, I am a warner to you, etc.

In his saying: "that you should worship Allah" (أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ), he called them to His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in His worship (العبادة), for the people were idolaters (وثنيين) who worshipped idols. Idolatry (الوثنية) does not permit the worship of Allah, the Glorified, neither alone nor with others. Rather, they worshipped the lords of the idols through the worship of the idols so that they might be intercessors (شفعاء) for them with Allah. Had they permitted His worship, the Exalted, they would have worshipped Him alone; thus, his calling them to the worship of Allah was a call to His monotheism in worship.

In his saying: "and be wary of Him" (وَاتَّقُوهُ), he called them to avoid His acts of disobedience (معاصيه), including major and minor sins, which range from polytheism (الشرك) to what is below it, and to perform righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) whose neglect constitutes disobedience.

In his saying: "and obey me" (وَأَطِيعُونِ), he called them to obey his own self, which necessitates believing in his mission and taking the landmarks of their religion (الدين) — *the divine system of beliefs and laws* — from him (peace be upon him), regarding how Allah, the Glorified, is worshipped and the way of life is conducted. Thus, in his saying: "Worship Allah, be wary of Him, and obey me," there is an invitation to the three principles of religion (أصول الدين): monotheism (التوحيد), indicated by his saying: "Worship Allah"; the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment* — which is the foundation of godwariness (التقوى) (1); and the affirmation of Prophethood (النبوة), indicated by the call to absolute obedience.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ** — "He will forgive you some of your sins" (71:4). The verb is in the jussive mood as the result of the command. The word "some" (مِنْ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض) according to what is apparent from the context. The meaning is: if you worship Him, be wary of Him, and obey me, He will forgive some of your sins, namely those committed before faith (الإيمان): polytheism and what is below it. As for sins not yet committed in the future, there is no meaning in forgiving them before they occur, nor is there meaning in promising to forgive them if they occur in the future, or whenever they occur, as that would entail the nullification of religious obligations (التكاليف الدينية) by nullifying the punishment for violating them.

This is supported by the literal meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "O our people! Respond to Allah’s inviter and have faith in Him. He will forgive you some of your sins" (46:31), and His saying: "He calls you that He may forgive you some of your sins" (14:10), and His saying: "Tell the faithless that if they desist, what is past will be forgiven them" (8:38).

As for His saying, the Exalted, addressing the believers (المؤمنين) of this community: "O you who have faith! Shall I show you a trade that will deliver you from a painful punishment? Have faith in Allah and His Apostle and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your possessions and your persons. That is better for you, should you know. He will forgive you your sins and admit you into gardens..." (61:10-12). Although this appears to mean the forgiveness of all sins, the forgiveness there is predicated upon the continuity of faith and righteous deeds and their maintenance throughout life. Thus, there is no forgiveness there pertaining to future acts of disobedience and sins that have not yet occurred, nor a promise to forgive them whenever they occur.

Some have leaned, relying on the generality of forgiveness in the verse of Surah al-Saff, toward the view that the sins forgiven due to faith in this community are all sins, while in other communities it was only some, as is apparent from Nuh's saying to his community: "He will forgive you some of your sins" (مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ), and the saying of the Messengers as in Surah Ibrahim: "He calls you that He may forgive you some of your sins," and the saying of the Jinn as in Surah al-Ahqaf to their people: "Respond to Allah’s inviter and have faith in Him. He will forgive you some of your sins."

The critique of this is that the verse in Surah al-Saff does not concern forgiveness solely due to faith, as we have pointed out. Furthermore, the verse in Surah al-Anfal is explicit regarding the forgiveness of "what is past," and those addressed are the disbelievers (كفار) of this community.

Some have held that "some" (مِنْ) in His saying "of your sins" (مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ) is extra (زائدة), but the addition of "min" in affirmative statements is not established, so this is a weak view. Equally weak is the view of those who held that it is explanatory (بيانية), or those who held it denotes the starting point of a limit (ابتداء الغاية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "and grant you respite until a specified time. Indeed, Allah’s [appointed] time cannot be deferred when it comes, should you know." (71:4). Predicating their respite until a specified time (أجل مسمى) upon the worship of Allah, godwariness, and obedience to the Messenger indicates that there are two terms: a specified term (أجل مسمى) to which Allah defers them if they respond to the call, and another term to which they are hastened if they remain in disbelief (الكفر). The specified term is the furthest and more distant of the two terms.

Thus, the verse promises them respite until the specified term if they believe. His saying: "Indeed, Allah’s time cannot be deferred when it comes" (إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ) serves as the rationale for the respite until the specified term if they believe. What is meant by "Allah's time when it comes" is the absolute decreed and inevitable term, whether it be the specified term or the non-specified one; for there is no turner of His decree (قضائه) and no over-ruler of His judgment.

The meaning is: Worship Allah, be wary of Him, and obey me, and Allah will defer you until a specified term, which is the further of the two terms. For if you do not do so, the non-specified term will come to you due to your disbelief and you will not be deferred, for Allah's time, when it comes, is not deferred. Thus, in the speech, in addition to the promise of respite until the specified term if they believe, there is a threat of an expedited punishment if they do not believe.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the interpretation by some of "Allah's time" as the non-specified term is incorrect, and even weaker is its interpretation as the specified term.

Some mentioned that "Allah's time" refers to the Day of Resurrection, and it appears they also interpret the "specified term" as the Day of Resurrection. The meaning of the verse would then be like saying: If you do not believe, Allah will hasten the punishment of this world upon you, and if you believe, He will defer you until the Day of Resurrection, which, when it comes, cannot be deferred.

You are well aware that this does not harmonize with the glad tidings in His saying: "He will forgive you some of your sins."

His saying: "should you know" (لَوْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ) relates to the beginning of the speech; i.e., if you knew that Allah has two terms and that His term, when it comes, is not deferred, you would have responded to my call, worshipped Allah, been wary of Him, and obeyed me. Thus, the object of "you know" (تَعْلَمُونَ) is omitted, indicated by the preceding speech.

It is also said that "you know" (تَعْلَمُونَ) is treated as an intransitive verb, and the result of "if" (لو) relates to the beginning of the speech, meaning: if you were people of knowledge, you would have responded to my call and believed. Or it relates to the end of the speech, meaning: if you were people of knowledge, you would know that Allah's time, when it comes, is not deferred.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِي لَيْلاً وَنَهاراً فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعائِي إِلَّا فِراراً** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed, I have called my people night and day, but my call only increased them in flight.’" (71:5-6). The speaker is Nuh (peace be upon him), and that which he called to was the worship of Allah, godwariness, and obedience to His Messenger. Calling "night and day" is a metaphor for its continuity without languor or negligence.

His saying: "but my call only increased them in flight" (فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعائِي إِلَّا فِراراً) means flight from responding to my call. What is meant by "flight" (الفرار) is rebellion and refusal to accept, used metaphorically. The attribution of the increase in flight to his call is due to the trace of causality (السببية) within it; for when good falls upon an unfit locus, the locus resists it with the corruption it contains, thereby corrupting it and turning it into evil. Allah has said regarding the description of the Quran: "We send down in the Quran that which is a healing and mercy for the believers, and it only increases the wrongdoers in loss" (17:82).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنِّي كُلَّما دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوا أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَاسْتَغْشَوْا ثِيابَهُمْ** — "‘Indeed, whenever I called them that You might forgive them, they put their fingers into their ears and drew their cloaks over themselves...’" (71:7) etc. He mentioned His forgiveness, the Exalted, as the goal of his call, whereas the original sense is: "I called them so they might believe, so that You might forgive them." This is because the intent is to indicate that he was sincere toward them in his call and desired nothing but the good of their worldly life and their hereafter.

His saying: "they put their fingers into their ears" (جَعَلُوا أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ) is a metaphor for their disdain for listening to his call. His saying: "and drew their cloaks over themselves" (وَاسْتَغْشَوْا ثِيابَهُمْ) means they covered their heads and faces with them so they would not see me nor hear my speech, which is a metaphor for aversion and the refusal to listen to his words.

His saying: "and they persisted and became disdainful with [utter] pride" (وَأَصَرُّوا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا اسْتِكْباراً) means they insisted on refusing to listen and were arrogant toward accepting my call with an extraordinary pride.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهاراً** — "‘Then I called them aloud.’" (71:8). "Then" (ثُمَّ) denotes a progression in the rank of the discourse. "Aloud" (الجهار) means calling with a raised voice.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ إِنِّي أَعْلَنْتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْراراً** — "‘Then I appealed to them publicly, and I spoke to them privately.’" (71:9). Public appeal (الإعلان) and private speaking (الإسرار) are opposites, meaning manifestation and concealment. The apparent context is that the referent of the pronoun "to them" (لهم) in both instances is the same. Thus, the meaning is: I called them secretly and openly, sometimes publicly and sometimes privately, pursuing every possible method in my call and traveling every hopeful path.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقُلْتُ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً** — to His saying — **أَنْهاراً** — "‘and I said, "Plead with your Lord for forgiveness; indeed, He is All-Forgiver. He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, and aid you with wealth and sons, and provide you with gardens and provide you with rivers."’" (71:10-12). He justified the command to seek forgiveness by saying: "indeed, He is All-Forgiver" (إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً), indicating that He, the Exalted, is abundant in forgiveness, and this, in addition to its abundance from Him, is a continuous sunnah (سنة) — *an established divine way or practice* — of His, the Exalted.

His saying: "He will send for you abundant rains from the sky" (يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً) is in the jussive mood as the result of the command. What is meant by "the sky" (السماء) is the clouds, and "abundant" (المدرار) means frequent in pouring rain.

His saying: "and aid you with wealth and sons" (وَيُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ). Aiding (الأمداد) is the providing of support (المدد), which is that by which the one aided is strengthened in his need. Wealth and sons are the closest primary supports by which human society seeks assistance for its vital needs.

His saying: "and provide you with gardens and provide you with rivers" (وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ أَنْهاراً). These are types of wealth, but because they are among the most fundamental necessities of livelihood, they were specifically mentioned.

The verses — as you see — enumerate worldly blessings and narrate from him (peace be upon him) that he promised his people an abundance and succession of blessings upon them if they sought forgiveness from their Lord. For the forgiveness of sins has a profound effect in removing calamities and general afflictions and in opening the gates of blessings from the heaven and the earth. That is, there is a specific connection between the righteousness or corruption of human society and the general cosmic conditions linked to human life and the goodness or misery of its existence.

This is also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned" (30:41), and His saying: "Whatever affliction visits you is because of what your hands have earned" (42:30), and His saying: "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (7:94). There has preceded in the commentary (تفسير) of these verses that which is not without benefit in this regard.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما لَكُمْ لا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقاراً** — "What is the matter with you that you do not hope for majesty from Allah," (71:13). This is a rhetorical question of denunciation (استفهام إنكاري). Majesty (الوقار) — as in *al-Majma‘* — means greatness (العظمة), a noun derived from *tawqir*, meaning veneration (التعظيم). Hope (الرجاء) is the opposite of fear (الخوف), and it is the expectation of that which contains pleasure. What is meant by it in the verse is absolute belief (الاعتقاد), according to what has been said; and it is said: what is meant by it is fear, due to the correlation between them.

The meaning is: What reason has occurred to you such that you do not believe in, or do not fear, a greatness for Allah that necessitates that you worship Him?

The truth is that "hope" (الرجاء) is intended in its well-known sense, which is the opposite of fear, and its negation is a metaphor for despair (اليأس). It is often used as a metaphor for it; it is said: "I do not hope for any good in him," meaning: "I am in despair that there is any good in him." Majesty (الوقار) is stability (الثبوت), settledness (الاستقرار), and empowerment (التمكن), which is the root of its meaning as stated in *al-Majma‘*. His majesty, the Exalted, is His stability and settledness in Lordship (الربوبية), which entails His Divinity (ألوهيته) and His being the object of worship (معبوديته).

It is as if the idolaters sought a lord who has stability in Lordship so they might worship him, but they despaired of Him, the Exalted, and thus worshipped others. This is indeed the case, for they see that our understandings cannot encompass Him, the Exalted, so there is no way to direct worship toward Him. Worship is the fulfillment of the right of Lordship, from which the management of affairs (تدبير الأمر) branches out; and the management of the affairs of the world is delegated to various classes of angels and jinn, so they are our lords whom we must worship so they may be intercessors for us with Allah. As for Him, the Exalted, He has nothing but origination (الإيجاد) — the origination of the lords and their subjects altogether — without the management of affairs.

The verse, namely His saying: "What is the matter with you that you do not hope for majesty from Allah," and what follows it until the completion of seven verses, is aimed at establishing His majesty, the Exalted, in Lordship, and is a decisive proof in refuting what they fabricated regarding the necessity of worshipping others such as angels and others based on the attribution of the world's management to them. Through it, the possibility of directing worship toward Him, the Exalted, becomes clear.

The summary of the proof is: What called you to negate His Lordship, the Exalted, which entails Divinity and being the object of worship, and to despair of His majesty? While you know that He, the Exalted, created you and created the world in which you live in a manner of creation (طوراً من الخلق) that is inseparable from this system running within it. The management of the cosmos and those within it, including man, is nothing but the created developments in its parts and the system running within it. Thus, His being the Creator, the Exalted, is His being the Owner and Manager; so He is the Lord (الرب), there is no lord besides Him, and He must be taken as the God (إلهاً) to be worshipped.

Through this, the correctness of directing oneself toward Him, the Exalted, in worship becomes clear; for we know Him by His noble attributes of creation, provision, mercy, and the rest of His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية). Thus, we may direct ourselves toward Him through what we know of His attributes (1).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْواراً** — "when He has created you in [various] stages?" (71:14). This is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the subject of "you do not hope." Stages (الأطوار) is the plural of *tawr*, which is the limit of a thing and the state it is in.

The summary of the meaning is: You do not hope for majesty for Allah in Lordship, while He has produced you stage after stage, one stage following another. He produced each one of you from dust, then a drop of fluid (نطفة), then a clinging mass (علقة), then a fleshy tissue (مضغة), then a fetus, then a child, then a youth, then an old man. And He produced your collective whole with diverse individuals in masculinity and femininity, colors, forms, strength, and weakness, among other things. Is this anything but management (التدبير)? Thus, He is the Manager of your affairs, and He is your Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللهُ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً** — "Have you not regarded how Allah has created the seven heavens in layers," (71:15). The layering (مطابقة) of the seven heavens one with another means some are above others, or their correspondence and similarity, according to the two possibilities mentioned in the commentary of the beginning of Surah al-Mulk.

What is meant by "regarding" (الرؤية) is knowledge. The description of the seven heavens — while the speech is presented as a proof — indicates that they used to see them as being seven and conceded that; thus, He argued against them using what was conceded by them.

In any case, the occurrence of the discourse on the seven heavens in the speech of Nuh is evidence of it being transmitted from the Prophets (peace be upon them) from the most ancient of times.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُوراً وَجَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِراجاً** — "and made the moon a light in them, and made the sun a lamp?" (71:16). The verses — as evidenced by their context — are aimed at explaining the occurrence of divine management over man through the blessings He bestows upon him, so that His Lordship is established and His worship becomes mandatory.

Based on this, the sun being a lamp (سراجاً) means it is the illuminator for our world, and were it not for it, we would be submerged in pitch darkness. The moon being a light (نُوراً) means it is the enlightener of our earth with a light acquired from the sun; it is not an illuminator by itself such that it would be considered a lamp.

As for taking the heavens as a container for the moon in His saying: "and made the moon a light in them" (فِيهِنَّ), what is meant by it, as has been said, is its being within their realm, even if it is in only one of them. This is like saying: "In these houses is a well," even if it is in only one of them, because what is in one of them is in them [collectively]; and like saying: "I came to the tribe of Tamim," when you only came to some of them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً** — "Allah has made you grow from the earth like a plant." (71:17). That is, He made you grow with the growth of plants. This is because the creation of man ends at earthly elements combined in a manner specific to him; he takes nourishment, grows, and produces his like. This is the reality of plants. Thus, the speech is presented as a reality without simile or metaphor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيها وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْراجاً** — "Then He will make you return into it, and He will bring you forth [once again]." (71:18). The return into it is through death and burial, and the bringing forth is for the recompense on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, this verse and the one before it are close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: "In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth" (7:25).

In His saying: "and He will bring you forth" (وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ), without saying "then He will bring you forth," there is an allusion that the return and the bringing forth are like a single act, and the return is a prelude to the bringing forth. Man, in the states of return and bringing forth, is in the Abode of Truth, just as in the world he is in the Abode of Delusion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ بِساطاً** — "Allah has made the earth a carpet for you," (71:19). That is, like a carpet, making it easy for you to move from side to side and to transfer from one region to another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِتَسْلُكُوا مِنْها سُبُلاً فِجاجاً** — "that you may traverse its broadways." (71:20). Ways (السبل) is the plural of *sabil*, meaning path. Broadways (الفجاج) is the plural of *fajj*, meaning a wide path; and it is said: a path located between two mountains.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَاتَّبَعُوا مَنْ لَمْ يَزِدْهُ مالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إِلَّا فِراراً** — "Noah said, ‘My Lord! They have disobeyed me, and followed someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss.’" (71:21). This is a return by him (peace be upon him) to his complaint about his people to his Lord, after having mentioned the details of his call to them and the speech he delivered to them from his saying: "Then I called them aloud" until the end of the verses.

His previous complaint was his saying: "but my call only increased them in flight," after he had reported the summary of his call by saying: "My Lord! Indeed, I have called my people night and day."

In the verse, there is an indication that the affluent grandees among his people were blocking people from him and inciting them to oppose and harm him.

The meaning of his saying: "whose wealth and children only add to his loss" — while wealth and children were counted in his previous speech among the blessings — is that the wealth and children, which are among Your blessings and for which they should have been grateful, only increased them in disbelief, and that bequeathed them loss (خسراناً) from Your mercy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَكَرُوا مَكْراً كُبَّاراً** — "They have devised a monstrous plot," (71:22). Monstrous (كُبَّاراً) is an intensive form of greatness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالُوا لا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَلا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلا سُواعاً وَلا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْراً** — "and said, ‘Do not abandon your gods. Do not abandon Wadd, nor Suwa‘, nor Yaghuth, Ya‘uq and Nasr.’" (71:23). This was a recommendation from them to cling to their gods and not to abandon their worship.

Wadd, Suwa‘, Yaghuth, Ya‘uq, and Nasr were five of their gods whom they were intensely concerned with worshipping; therefore, they specified them by name along with the recommendation regarding gods in general. Perhaps the prefixing of Wadd and the mention of Suwa‘ and Yaghuth with the negative particle "la" (لا) for emphasis is because they were of greater importance to them than Ya‘uq and Nasr. Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا كَثِيراً وَلا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلالاً** — "‘They have already led many astray. Do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but error.’" (71:24). The pronoun in "they have led astray" (أَضَلُّوا) refers to the followed leaders, and this is supported by the fact that they are the ones spoken of in His saying: "and they devised" and "and they said: Do not abandon your gods." It is also said: the pronoun refers to the idols, for they are the ones who lead astray, but this is not without some distance.

His saying: "Do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but error" (وَلا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلالاً) is a supplication from Nuh against the wrongdoers (الظالمين) for error. What is meant by it is error as a recompense (مجازاة), rather than initial error. Thus, it is a supplication from him that Allah recompense them for their disbelief and transgression, in addition to what will be narrated of his supplication against them for destruction.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: "Allah, the Glorified, has made seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) a cause for the abundance of provision and mercy for the creation. For the Glorified said: ‘Plead with your Lord for forgiveness; indeed, He is All-Forgiver. He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, and aid you with wealth and sons.’ So may Allah have mercy on a man who faces his repentance, seeks the annulment of his sin, and anticipates his death."

**I say:** The narrations regarding the derivation of the causality of seeking forgiveness for the expansion of provision and being aided with children from these verses are numerous.

In *al-Khisal*, narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred": "Increase seeking forgiveness, for it attracts provision."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "What is the matter with you that you do not hope for majesty from Allah," he said: "You do not fear greatness for Allah."

**I say:** This meaning has also been narrated through Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas.

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "seven heavens in layers," he said: "meaning some above others."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Noah said, ‘My Lord! They have disobeyed me, and followed someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss,’" he said: "They followed the wealthy."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bukhari, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The idols and statues that were among the people of Nuh later became among the Arabs. As for Wadd, it belonged to Kalb in Dawmat al-Jandal. As for Suwa‘, it belonged to Hudhayl. As for Yaghuth, it belonged to Murad, then to the Banu Ghutayf at Saba’. As for Ya‘uq, it belonged to Hamdan. As for Nasr, it belonged to Himyar for the family of Dhu al-Kala‘. They were the names of righteous men from the people of Nuh. When they perished, Satan inspired their people: ‘Erect statues in the assemblies where they used to sit and name them by their names.’ So they did so, but they were not worshipped until those [who erected them] perished and knowledge was abrogated; then they were worshipped."

**I say:** Perhaps what is meant by those idols that belonged to the people of Nuh "becoming" among the Arabs is the correspondence of what the Arabs had with what they had in names, or in descriptions and names. As for the transfer of those idols in their physical persons to the Arabs, it is extremely far-fetched.

The story was also narrated in *‘Ilal al-Shara’i‘* with its chain to Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him) as in the narration.

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain to al-Mufaddal from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "Nuh built his ark in the Mosque of Kufa with his own hand, and the wood was brought from afar until he finished it." He said: "Then he turned to his left and pointed with his hand to the location of the house of the Dariyyin — which is the location of the house of Ibn Hakim, and that is the Euphrates today — and he said to me: ‘O Mufaddal, here were erected the idols of the people of Nuh: Yaghuth, Ya‘uq, and Nasr.’"
